subject: science

primary.justification: The first half of the chapter (sections 5.1–5.4) is centrally about measurement of length. It explains why non-standard body-part units (handspan, cubit, stride) are unreliable, introduces the SI system and the metre as the standard unit, establishes sub-units (cm, mm) and the larger unit km with their conversion relationships, and teaches the correct technique for using a scale — placement, eye position, and handling a broken scale. This maps precisely to C-1.3's mandate to explain the importance of measurement and measure physical properties of matter in indigenous, non-standard, and standard units using simple instruments.

The second half of the chapter (sections 5.5–5.7) is architecturally independent and centrally about describing motion. It introduces the reference point as the basis for describing position, defines motion as change of position with respect to a reference point over time, and classifies motion into three types — linear (straight-line path), circular (circular path), and oscillatory (to-and-fro about a fixed position). This directly addresses C-2.1's requirement to describe one-dimensional and path-characterised motion using physical measurements of position.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to measure length accurately using standard SI units and to describe and classify motion by identifying how an object's position changes relative to a reference point over time.
